name: Residential sensing architecture expansion strategy
consequences: Immediate: Sensing architecture expansion yields higher event capture during nights with fewer missed episodes. Systemic: Data volume grows by 35% and processing costs rise by 20% annually due to the additional sensors and cloud-linked analytics; Trade-off: richer data improves phenotyping but increases privacy management burden. Strategic: Enables scalable, higher-quality longitudinal phenotyping with capacity for cross-site collaboration, but requires stronger governance and budget discipline.
options: Maintain eight active suites with incremental, non-disruptive sensor upgrades to preserve the domestic feel.
Expand to twelve suites using enhanced mattress sensors and dry-electrode EEG with standardized scoring throughput, while maintaining existing privacy controls.
Adopt a cloud-linked modular sensor stack with data-as-a-service partnerships to scale to twenty suites and enable remote expert triage.
review: Controls data richness vs. privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory alignment across EU privacy frameworks and data sharing constraints.

name: Participant recruitment and retention framework
consequences: Immediate: Outreach efforts accelerate enrollment velocity in the initial weeks. Systemic: Enrollment rate increases by 40% and per-participant recruitment cost rises by 12% due to broader outreach. Trade-off: faster enrollment versus budget discipline and consent tracking complexity. Strategic: Shortens time-to-Aim 2 milestones and improves external credibility, but increases governance and consent-tracking complexity.
options: Maintain Bonn-centric pre-admission screening with enhanced local referrals.
Expand recruitment to regional neurologists and patient networks with standardized compensation and streamlined consent.
Launch a data-escrow-driven multi-site recruitment model with paid access to de-identified screening datasets for researchers and a dynamic consent framework.
review: Controls enrollment speed vs budget discipline. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border ethical approvals and consent management complexity.

name: Event-triage tooling development and validation
consequences: Immediate: Triage backlog decreases as manual review is accelerated. Systemic: Annotation throughput rises by 30%, but inter-rater reliability declines by 3–5 percentage points absent calibration; Trade-off: speed vs consistency. Strategic: Improves scalability and external collaboration potential but increases governance and access controls complexity.
options: Refine manual scoring workflows and maintain dual independent scoring to ensure validity.
Deploy semi-automated triage using validated algorithms to prioritize reviews and cut manual load by 30%.
Develop a cloud-enabled event-triage engine with continuous learning from adjudicated episodes and offer controlled external research access on a pay-use basis.
review: Controls triage speed vs scoring consistency. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory constraints on external data access and data-use agreements.

name: Data governance and privacy architecture
consequences: Immediate: De-identification and access controls slow data processing slightly but strengthen privacy. Systemic: Privacy controls reduce re-identification risk by 80% with a 25% increase in data preparation time and 10% higher storage costs due to metadata management; Trade-off: stronger privacy reduces speed-to-analysis and increases overhead. Strategic: Builds trust and enables broader collaborations, but requires robust governance and regulatory compliance.
options: Tighten de-identification protocols and maintain existing on-site storage with offline backups.
Introduce dynamic consent and privacy-preserving analytics with differential privacy-inspired techniques.
Establish a participant-owned data escrow with tiered access and a subscription-based data access model for external researchers.
review: Controls privacy protection vs data usability. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-jurisdictional data sharing ethics and consent governance.

name: Staffing model and operational risk management
consequences: Immediate: Coverage efficiency improves and overtime burden declines. Systemic: Overtime costs down 12%, but fatigue risk increases by 2–3 percentage points if mispredictions occur; Trade-off: automation reduces cost but may erode safety margin. Strategic: Enables scalable operations but demands ongoing governance and validation.
options: Maintain current staffing schedule with safety margins and cross-training.
Increase cross-training, add on-call incentives, and implement formal safety drills.
Introduce a ML-informed scheduling platform to optimize coverage, reduce overtime, and implement proactive safety dashboards.
review: Controls staffing safety vs cost efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of over-reliance on automation and the need for continual independent oversight.

name: Funding and collaboration model
consequences: Immediate: Access to new funds accelerates project milestones. Systemic: Budget increases by 60% with 15% higher administrative burden and two-week decision latency. Trade-off: more funding enables expansion but slows governance and strategic alignment. Strategic: Ensures long-term viability and wider research impact but may threaten academic autonomy.
options: Maintain sole reliance on DFG and University of Bonn internal funding with strict expenditure controls.
Pursue external pharmaceutical partnership with defined publication windows and cost-sharing.
Create a multi-institution research consortium with tiered sponsorship, shared governance, and a data access/subscription framework to fund expansion.
review: Controls funding diversity vs project autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-institution governance harmonization and IP allocation complexities.

name: Parasomnia event triage accuracy optimization
consequences: Immediate: Integrates multi-modal data sources to yield a single, coherent triage signal for suspected parasomnia events. → Systemic: False-positive rate decreases by 20% and annotation throughput increases by 25%, but per-participant monitoring costs rise by 12%. → Strategic: The project gains faster decision cycles and more reliable phenotyping but faces budget pressure and greater operational complexity as data modalities scale.
options: Standardize event tagging to PSG-confirmed cues and require dual human scoring for all events.
Introduce layered adjudication combining dry-electrode data with video review and probabilistic scoring to triage events for manual review.
Implement privacy-preserving edge analytics to produce real-time triage scores and form an external consortium for federated, de-identified event benchmarking.
review: Controls throughput vs accuracy in event triage. Weakness: The options fail to consider data privacy constraints for real-time edge analytics.

name: Privacy-forward in-room data governance for parasomnia research
consequences: Immediate: Privacy-preserving measures elevate confidentiality by restricting identifiability of in-room data. → Systemic: De-identified data sharing expands collaboration by 40% of potential partners, yet data processing cycles grow 15% and some granular analyses become less feasible. → Strategic: The approach broadens funding opportunities and external validation but increases governance overhead and requires ongoing privacy/compliance staffing.
options: Institute strict access controls and anonymization for any in-room video data, with quarterly privacy audits.
Implement a consent framework with opt-in granular controls and dynamic re-consent triggers based on new data modalities.
Adopt differential privacy-enabled on-device video abstraction to share de-identified summaries with external partners under time-bound licenses.
review: Controls privacy vs data utility in in-room data sharing. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential regulatory pushback on on-device video processing.

name: EEG-BIDS cross-site data harmonization and archival acceleration
consequences: Immediate: Establishes standardized EEG-BIDS and cross-site data structures enabling interoperability. → Systemic: Time-to-access reduces by 40% and cross-site collaboration increases by 30%, but data governance complexity grows by 25% and requires additional staff. → Strategic: Elevates the unit as a reusable data asset hub with higher IP considerations and governance overhead.
options: Maintain current NAS storage with manual metadata curation and local backups.
Automate EEG-BIDS tagging and implement encrypted replication to a central data lake with standardized metadata schemas.
Launch a private, on-site data marketplace with partner institutions to monetize de-identified datasets under time-bound access, with automated provenance tracking.
review: Controls data standardization vs cross-site interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider multi-site regulatory alignment and cross-border data-sharing governance.

name: Adaptive stay-optimization and admission pacing for yield stability
consequences: Immediate: Reduces unproductive nights and aligns stay length with event likelihood. → Systemic: Occupancy efficiency improves by 18%, but event capture per participant declines by 10% if screening excludes borderline cases. → Strategic: Improves financial viability and throughput but narrows eligibility and introduces external dependencies.
options: Tighten inclusion criteria and pre-admission screening to minimize unproductive stays.
Introduce adaptive scheduling with rolling admissions and mid-stay reassessment to maximize event capture.
Sell capacity as a modular research service to external projects via a rotating guest-ward model, aligning with a shared-risk, revenue-sharing framework.
review: Controls stay utilization vs event capture yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider capacity constraints and potential patient cohort attrition during expansion.

name: Staffing and training model for scalable annotation throughput
consequences: Immediate: Improves annotation throughput and reduces fatigue-driven errors. → Systemic: Throughput up 40% and labor costs up 15% due to on-demand staffing; scheduling becomes more complex. → Strategic: Increases scalability and resilience but adds governance complexity and IP concerns.
options: Cross-train existing staff for annotation throughput improvements.
Introduce night-coverage telepresence support with remote scorers to increase throughput while managing fatigue.
Create a modular, performance-based staffing pool with a shared, on-demand night-operations hub that partners with external researchers under an open-science, revenue-sharing framework.
review: Controls staffing flexibility vs annotation consistency. Weakness: The options fail to consider staff burnout and retention under more variable schedules.

name: Collaborations and funding diversification through tiered partnerships
consequences: Immediate: Diversifies funding streams and reduces reliance on a single grant. → Systemic: Per-participant revenue increases by 20%, but industry obligations may limit publication freedom by 10%. → Strategic: Expands impact and sustainability but requires stronger governance on conflicts of interest and IP.
options: Maintain core DFG/University funding with milestone-driven deliverables.
Secure industry partnerships with explicit publication rights and IP terms.
Establish a data-analytic services subscription model for external clinics, funded by outcomes-based payments.
review: Controls collaboration flexibility vs publication autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory and IP risk interactions across industry partnerships.

name: Harmonizing monitoring modalities for data richness and participant comfort
consequences: Immediate: Expansion of low-burden sensing increases near-continuous data capture with minimal participant discomfort. Systemic: Data completeness rises by 12 percentage points and retention improves by 4 points, but nightly operating cost per participant climbs by 8%, reflecting a data richness vs cost trade-off. Strategic: This enables deeper phenotyping and scalable enrollment, yet increases governance and logistics complexity.
options: Scale up low-burden sensing while maintaining strict consent and privacy guardrails.
Introduce scheduled enhanced nights with partial PSG, balancing data yield and participant fatigue and piloting opt-ins.
Deploy privacy-preserving on-device analytics with federated analytics architecture and a data-sharing subscription model for collaborating centers.
review: Controls data richness vs participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider evolving privacy regulation and consent drift risk for long-term cross-site data collection.

name: Privacy-preserving longitudinal data sharing and cross-site governance
consequences: Immediate: Tighter privacy controls reduce risk of consent violations. Systemic: Cross-site harmonization improves inter-rater reliability by 6 percentage points and data-sharing throughput by 25%, but governance overhead consumes an additional 9% of the budget. Strategic: Enables broader external validation but slows initial data access and requires sustained governance.
options: Limit data to de-identified, site-local datasets with rigid access controls.
Establish a federated analytics framework where raw data never leaves the site and only aggregated summaries are shared.
Launch a privacy-preserving federated analytics platform with cross-site access under a regional data-sharing consortium funded by a subscription revenue model.
review: Controls privacy vs cross-site collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border data transfer regulatory risk and GDPR nuances with non-EU partners.

name: Retention- and recruitment-optimization through participant experience design
consequences: Immediate: Enhanced onboarding reduces early attrition and shortens recruitment cycle by 2 weeks on average. Systemic: Enrollment completion rises by 15 percentage points and stay-duration compliance improves by 10 percentage points, but compensation and micro-grants costs rise by about 12%. Strategic: Builds participant loyalty and data richness but binds the program to ongoing financial commitments and governance structures.
options: Extend pre-admission screening and onboarding to reduce attrition and align expectations.
Increase compensation modestly and provide flexible scheduling to reduce dropout risk.
Create a regional participant consortium with co-designed admission processes and a micro-grant fund, backed by a regional sponsorship model that licenses forecasting tools to partner centers.
review: Controls recruitment speed vs participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term financial sustainability of incentive-based recruitment and potential bias from compensation levels.

name: Nightly event-triage orchestration and workflow integration
consequences: Immediate: Triage speed improves as rule-based ranking reduces manual review load. Systemic: Cycle time for adjudication drops by 40%, but false-positive rate increases by 2–3 percentage points. Strategic: Accelerates iteration and throughput, but risks reduced specificity; requires monitoring and calibration.
options: Maintain manual review with dual scoring on all events.
Automate triage ranking with rule-based heuristics and maintain human oversight.
Deploy privacy-preserving federated triage engine with on-site inference and external benchmarking, plus a cross-site triage service via a subscription model.
review: Controls triage speed vs accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential over-reliance on automation and erosion of expert review skills.

name: Safe-by-design facility expansion and risk mitigation
consequences: Immediate: Safety measures yield faster incident detection and response readiness. Systemic: Capital expenditures rise by 15% and ongoing maintenance by 6%, but liability exposure decreases by 20%. Strategic: Strengthens resilience and regulatory confidence, enabling scale, but introduces vendor and insurer risk.
options: Maintain current safety features and staff training; conduct annual audits.
Increase staffing margins and implement staged escalation thresholds and quarterly drills.
Implement autonomous incident detection with edge analytics and risk-sharing partnerships with insurers.
review: Controls safety vs cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider integration risk with hospital systems and reliability of external safety vendors.

name: Inter-rater reliability augmentation and scoring standardization
consequences: Immediate: Scoring calibration improves as new criteria are applied. Systemic: Inter-rater agreement increases from 0.80 to 0.88, but training time and governance overhead rise by ~5%. Strategic: Improves data quality and cross-site comparability for publications; however onboarding complexity increases and may slow initial recruitment.
options: Maintain current two-rated scoring with quarterly calibration.
Institute blinded cross-site double scoring and adjudication panel.
Adopt semi-automatic scoring augmentation via an on-site learning module with external benchmarking and accreditation.
review: Controls scoring consistency vs calibration time. Weakness: The options fail to consider training resource constraints and scorer turnover.
